column22: University of Leeds guarantees university accommodation for international students. If you will be studying at University of Leeds for semester 1 only, University of Leeds will offer you an accommodation contract that finishes during the middle of the January exam period.

column42: You may apply for an exchange abroad to the University of Leeds for either a single semester or a full academic year. This agreement allows for enrolment in undergraduate and postgraduate modules. University of Copenhagen MA-students considering taking undergraduate modules only - or a mix of undergraduate and postgraduate modules at the Univeristy of Leeds must make sure that they can get approval from their faculty to transfer undergraduate (bachelor) modules from the host university into their programmes here at UCPH. MA-students from the Faculty of Humanities should note that they cannot get undergraduate modules from this university transfered into their MA-programmes at UCPH.
column212: Semester 1 / autumn semester Even if semester 1 ends in January, Leeds cannot produce an official transcript of records (karakterudskrift) until the end of July. This is because the January exam results remain provisional until external exam board meetings in late June. University of Leeds will instead provide a provisional transcript for all students who are studying at Leeds for semester 1. This may delay your final transfer of credits at UCPH, as not all faculties accept provisional transcript of records for credit transfer.
column213: Academic transcripts for postgraduate modules are not released until November after studies have ended at Leeds. This means, students going on exchange in e.g the autumn semester, will not be able to transfer credits earned at Leeds before November in the subsequent academic year.
column214: If you choose a mixture of undergraduate and postgraduate modules, Leeds will send you a transcript in July, but the postgraduate results will be provisional until November and could change. Leeds will automatically send you another transcript in November, which will show all your official results. Please also note that provisional marks/results rarely change.

column55: The standard admissions requirement is IELTS 6.0 with not less than 5.5 in any one component or a corresponding result form another accepted test - see the list below. Country specific tests: Danish STX/HF/HHX or HTX with min 7 for English results. English B corresponds to level B2 on the CEFR-scale, which is accepted by Manchester as a 6.0 IELTS result. English A corresponds to level C1 on the CEFR-scala. For students planning to study at University of Manchester for 1 semester the following documents are required: 1) a transcript of records from high school with English on level B2 on the ECFR scale and 2) a document from the university confirming a level of English B2 on the CEFR scale. For students planning to study at University of Manchester for a full academic year must supply the same documents as stated above with one of the following: IELTS Academic (UKVI or non-UKVI), TOEFL iBT Home Edition, Pearson Test of English - Academic, Trinity College London Integrated Skills in English (ISE I, ISE II, ISE III), Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English (CAE) and Certificate in Proficiency in English (CPE), International Baccalaureate, Michigan Cambridge Language Assessments (CaMLA): Examination for the Certificate of Proficiency in English (ECPE), LanguageCert (UKVI version). Besides the general language requirements stated above students must also meet any equivalent subject area language requirements (where these are higher) to access units in those subject areas. Subject area requirements can be found under the relevant listing in the course unit search on the webpage: https://www.manchester.ac.uk/study/international/study-abroad-exchange/unit-search/. Please note that no Humanities subject areas accepts a score of lower than IELTS 6.5 or equivalent. If a student’s degree programme is taught entirely in English, Manchester will also accept a letter on headed paper from the home institution confirming that this is the case.
column160: The University of Manchester is part of the prestigious Russell Group of universitites, with outstanding facilities and a wide range of courses. They are highly respected across the globe as a centre of teaching excellence and innovative research.
column216: Law students can enrol onto undergraduate level courses 1,2 and 3, but cannot enrol onto Master’s level courses. column218: Most Level 3 course units are heavily oversubscribed, particularly Modern History courses. Students wishing to access Level 2 History courses should also have sufficient background study in the subject. History majors who plan to join us for Semester 2 of the academic year and who wish to take Level 3 History course units need to make an early application. Manchester recommends applying by 1 June.

column4:
Established in 1853, the University of Melbourne is Australia's second oldest university. The University of Melbourne is a public-spirited institution that makes distinctive contributions to society in research, teaching and learning, and engagement. It is consistently ranked among the leading universities in the world, with international rankings of world universities placing it as number 1 in Australia and number 37 in the world (Times Higher Education World University Rankings 2024). Melbourne offers broad undergraduate degrees and world-leading graduate programs to about 54,000 students each year, 44% being international students from 150+ countries.

Campus is located in the heart of Melbourne and there are currently over 200 clubs affiliated to the University of Melbourne Student Union (UMSU). Consistently voted as one of the world’s most livable cities, Melbourne is the cultural and sporting capital of Australia with many festivals and sporting events held throughout the year. With 38% of the population born overseas and 140 cultures represented, Melbourne is extremely multi-cultural and welcoming of people from all backgrounds. Melbourne’s food scene is world class and boasts more coffee shops per capita than any other city in the world!

column4: The University of Milan stands as a leading institution in Italy, recognized for its substantial investments in research infrastructure and fostering human capital. With the ever-changing landscape of knowledge, these two elements are crucial in addressing complex societal and industrial challenges. The university comprises eight faculties and two schools, encompassing a wide range of disciplines such as Agricultural and Food Sciences, Exercise and Sports Sciences, Humanities, Law, Linguistic and Cultural Mediation Sciences, Medicine, Pharmacy, Political, Economic & Social Sciences, Science & Technology, and Veterinary Medicine. In terms of student life, the university offers opportunities for engagement and enrichment beyond the classroom. The University Sports Centre (CUS) serves as an amateur sports association that has been promoting and organizing university sporting activities for over 70 years. Students have access to a variety of sports at affordable rates, both within the center's events and in affiliated sports facilities. Moreover, the university has established a network of relationships and signed agreements with cultural institutions and associations in Milan and other cities. This allows for vibrant cultural exchanges between the academic and local communities, providing students with discounts at museums, theaters, cinemas, and more, further enhancing their educational experience. Through its commitment to research, education, and the holistic development of its students, the University of Milan remains at the forefront of academic excellence and actively contributes to the intellectual, cultural, and social fabric of both Milan and the wider academic community.

column4: The university was established in 1949 and supports a community of more than 59,000 students, around 15,000 being international students from 120+ countries. With over 63,000 students and a vibrant exchange program, UNSW offers a dynamic academic experience enriched by strong industry connections and a global outlook. The university is committed to shaping forward thinking, environmentally conscious and socially engaged students who will make a positive impact in the world.  The main UNSW campus is located at Kensington, seven kilometers from the center of Sydney. UNSW Student Life (Arc) offers 3000 clubs to get social, get active and make the most of the time at UNSW. Located in the middle of China's coast (2.5 hours by bus from Shanghai), it is the cradle of the cultures in Yangtze River Delta and also a portal city equipped with modern port facilities and airport. Now it's playing an important role in economy, society, culture etc. as a major port city along the south-eastern coast, a economic center in the southern wing of Yangtze Delta and a famous state-level historic and cultural city. A lot of relics are well preserved here, representing the toil and wisdom of the ancestors.
All study programmes are delivered entirely in English.

column4: The University of Oslo is Norway’s oldest and largest university, founded in 1811, and today home to around 27,000 students. There are more than 800 courses taught in English across all faculties, making it quite accessible for exchange students. The university is spread across several campuses: the major one is Blindern, but there are additional facilities at places like Ullevål and Tøyen. Oslo, the capital of Norway, offers a unique combination of urban life and natural beauty, making it an attractive destination for students. The city is surrounded by forests and fjords, with outdoor activities like hiking, skiing, and kayaking easily accessible via public transport. Oslo is known for its cultural diversity, vibrant arts scene, and commitment to sustainability. Students can enjoy museums, music festivals, and a lively café culture, all within a compact and walkable city center.

column4: Nau mai, haere mai ki Ōtākou Whakaihu Waka – a globally recognised university nestled among the natural beauty of New Zealand’s spectacular South Island. The University of Otago is Aotearoa New Zealand’s first university, founded in 1869, and is the top university in the country for educational performance (TEC Educational Performance Indicators). Our Dunedin campus is recognised as one of the world’s most-beautiful campuses, with impressive historic and modern architecture, situated just a short walk from the main city centre. Dunedin is Australasia’s only true student city, offering an unmatched student experience with over 20,000 students living, working, and studying in Dunedin, creating an energy and atmosphere you will only find at Otago. Experience a true “kiwi” student lifestyle in the lively neighbourhoods surrounding campus, living as a community in Uniflats, self-catering flats. Otago is ranked #1 in the country for international student learning, with high student satisfaction rates, and world-class research-led teaching. For the adventure seeking student, experience the duality of study and travel that is highly appealing and accessible in Otago. Join our community of international students from more than 100 countries! Discover the University of Otago through the eyes of our international students!

The School of Engineering (Escola Politécnica) requires exchange students to enroll in a number of subjects that correspond to 12 credits per semester.
The Ribeirão Preto School of Economics, Administration, and Accounting (Faculdade de Economia e Administração de Ribeirão Preto – FEARP) requires applicants to enroll in a number of subjects that correspond to at least 16 credits.
The School of Architecture and Urbanism (Faculdade de Arquitetura e Urbanismo – FAU) and the School of Economics, Business, and Accountancy (Faculdade de Economia, Administração, Contabilidade e Atuária – FEA) receive a large number of applications each semester and have limited capacity to admit all exchange students. Please refer to their Local International Offices before nominating students/applying. It is highly recommended that students intending to apply for the above-mentioned schools consider the possibility of applying to other schools as well.

column40:
To be eligible for a KU exchange spot, students must have completed at least one year of study (equivalent to 60 ECTS) by the time they apply for admission to the host university. This must be documented on their transcript by the time of applying to the host university.

First-year bachelor students should note that applications to the host university are submitted before the start of each semester — typically between March and April for the autumn semester and between September and November for the spring semester.

First-year master's students are considered to meet this requirement based on their completed bachelor's degree.
column42: At the Brazilian postgraduate level, the so-called stricto sensu postgraduate programs are divided into two levels: mestrado (master's degree) and doutorado (doctoral/PhD degree). These programs may differ in structure from those offered abroad. Therefore, international students enrolled in subjects whose curricula at their home institutions are structured differently, such as Bologna second-cycle (Master's) degrees, are highly recommended to attend courses at USP preferably at the undergraduate level.

column4: The University of Stavanger is a mid-size public research university with around 13,000 students. It offers a wide range of programmes through its six main faculties covering most disciplines. While Norwegian is the main language of instruction, many courses especially at the master’s level are taught in English. Stavanger is a lively, coastal city in southwest Norway, combining urban amenities with nearby natural beauty. The Ullandhaug campus is about a 12-minute bus ride from the city centre, making it fairly accessible, and there are also good cycling options and public buses that connect campuses and residential areas. In your free time you can explore fjords, beaches, hiking paths, forests nearby, and enjoy cafés, shops and cultural events in Stavanger itself.

column4:
The University of Sydney is a world-leading institution committed to academic excellence and groundbreaking research. As Australia’s first university, it offers a rich tradition of innovation and intellectual discovery across a broad spectrum of disciplines. Exchange students benefit from access to inspiring teaching, state-of-the-art facilities, and a supportive learning environment designed to help you reach your full potential. With a strong global outlook and vibrant campus life, the University of Sydney fosters connections that extend far beyond the classroom, enriching your academic journey and future career.
Studying in Sydney provides an unparalleled lifestyle experience that complements your academic pursuits. With a student community of over 70,000 individuals, the university offers a diverse and welcoming environment where you can connect with peers from all over the world. Sydney itself is a thriving global city, known for its iconic landmarks, stunning beaches, and vibrant cultural scene. On campus, you’ll find more than 200 student clubs and societies spanning interests from sports and arts to academic and cultural groups, providing endless opportunities to engage, grow, and make lasting friendships. This dynamic blend of city life and campus community ensures your exchange experience will be as enriching personally as it is academically.

The university was founded in 1846 and supports a community of more than 35,000 students, 7,000 being international students from all over the world. Become an exchange student at the University of Tasmania and take advantage of Tasmania's unique and stunning environment. With 45% of our island State being National Park or World Heritage listed, it is no wonder that some of Tasmania's most breathtaking destinations, including Cradle Mountain and Tasman National Park, feature prominently in the Lonely Planet's 1000 Ultimate Adventures Guide.
Stunning beaches to surf, mountains to climb and rivers to raft, Tasmania the home of the Tassie Devil and the gateway to Antarctica will become your living playground, with the university being the epicentre of your state-sized class room.
With exceptional teaching staff excelling in areas including Zoology, Geography and Environmental studies, Marine and Antarctic science, Law, Aboriginal studies, Journalism and Media, combined with Summer rock concerts, Winter festivals and an art and cafe scene that will 'knock your socks off', the University of Tasmania can offer you an inspirational and diverse study experience, both enriching academically, socially, culturally and environmentally.

column4: In 1988, the former New South Wales Institute of Technology, founded in 1964, became the University of Technology Sydney. UTS is one of the largest universities in Australia with a total enrolment of over 45,000+ students. Study in award-winning labs and collaborative classrooms and work with the tools and technologies that are shaping your industry. Each year we welcome more than 1000 study abroad and exchange students from around the world – just like you. With students from 120 different countries, UTS is an exciting place to be!   The main campus, City Campus, is located right next to Central station and Victoria Park. UTS is part of the Australian Technology Network of universities: a group of five prominent universities committed to working with industry and government to deliver practical and professional courses.   See what it is like studying abroad at the University of Technology Sydney

- Students planning on starting the first semester of their Master's degree at UTS: In order to enroll in MA level courses you need to provide a transcript with 180 ECTS at the time of application or the latest shortly before semester start. You can not be guaranteed that you will be able to take MA courses.

- Subject approval and final enrolment into subjects is conditional upon the student meeting prerequisites, completion of the online enrolment process, and class availabilities at the time of enrolment. UTS cannot guarantee enrolment into subjects.

column4: The University of the Faroe Islands is a small but respected institution with about 1000 students. The university offers programs and courses mainly in Faroese and Danish, with some courses available in English. Its size and focus on regional issues provide a distinctive opportunity to study topics related to sustainability, marine resources, and the arctic region. The university is located in Tórshavn, the capital of the Faroe Islands, a small and charming city that combines traditional Nordic character with modern conveniences. The city is compact and quite hilly and offers a warm and friendly atmosphere. Surrounded by stunning cliffs, green valleys, and the open sea, the city provides easy access to hiking trails and breathtaking nature, making it a perfect setting for students who enjoy the outdoors.

column4: The University of Tokyo, also known as "Todai" was established in 1877 as the first national university in Japan. As a leading research university, Todai offers courses in essentially all academic disciplines at both undergraduate and graduate levels and conducts research across the full spectrum of academic activity. The university aims to provide its students with a rich and varied academic environment that ensures opportunities for both intellectual development and the acquisition of professional knowledge and skills.
column12: Fall semester: September/October to March* (transcripts are issued in April) Spring semester: April to August/September* (transcripts are issued in September) *The actual period of study (including examination periods) varies depending on the College/Faculty. Academic Calendar (u-tokyo.ac.jp) Important! UTokyo usually issues transcripts in September (after Spring exchange) and in April (after Fall exchange), which is later than other universities. Please make sure this will not conflict with your subsequent plans, particularly if the exchange is in your final semester of your bachelor's degree, as this will affect the timing of your credit transfer.
column15: Type U (For Undergraduate) - English: A minimum TOEFL iBT score of 90, IELTS 6.5, Cambridge "C1 Advanced", or DET 120 and/or - Japanese: JLPT N1. Students who wish to take courses taught in Japanese need JLPT Level N1. The different departments may have different requirements for the minimum level. Students who want to take courses taught in Japanese should check very early in the application process when there are available test dates for the JLPT in Denmark. There may only be 2 annual tests (typically December and July). It can be necessary to sign up for a test before you know, whether you have been awarded an exchange spot at University of Tokyo to make sure that you will have the test result in time for the application deadline at University of Tokyo. Type G (For Graduate): Graduate students must refer to the language requirement for the graduate school they wish to apply to: Prerequisites for USTEP Type G Applicants (u-tokyo.ac.jp). If there is no specified GPA requirement for the graduate school you intend to apply for, please note that University of Tokyo prefers students with a higher GPA and excellent academic performance.
column18: First and second year undergraduate students are not accepted for USTEP. For students enrolled in three-year undergraduate programs, they must have finished at least 1.5-year (3 semesters) of their degree programme at the time of their arrival at UTokyo. Students are advised to check the course catalogue about which language their preferred courses are taught in. Type G applicants must in addition to the general requirements also meet the conditions set by the Graduate School they intend to apply for: Prerequisites for USTEP Type G Applicants (u-tokyo.ac.jp)

column21: Exchange students can apply to the USTEP Type U (undergraduate) or USTEP Type G (graduate) program depending on their purpose of participation and/or their proficiency in the English and/or Japanese language. Type U is designed for undergraduate students to take various courses given in English and/or Japanese in the various Faculties/College in accordance with their English and/or Japanese language proficiency. Type G is designed for graduate students. Type G participants will belong to one of the Graduate Schools to which they are admitted and will be able to take various courses or conduct research under the guidance of a faculty advisor. Type G is only open to graduate students. Important information for graduate students: Type G applicants are expected to submit a detailed and well-developed study plan to UTokyo, ensuring that it is well aligned with the course offerings of the intended Graduate School and the research expertise of the department and faculty member(s). Failure to do so may result in rejection. Graduate students should only apply for this agreement, if they are able to develop an excellent study plan that meets the expectations described above, or if they are able to obtain a pre-approval of undergraduate courses abroad as part of their graduate degree program at UCPH, in which case, they should consider applying as Type U exchange students.

column4: The University of Toronto was founded in 1827 and supports a community of over 90,000 graduate- and undergraduate students. UofT has three campuses: St. George (UTSG), Mississauga (UTM) and Scarborough (UTSC). The UTM campus is the second-largest division of UofT and is situated 33 kilometres west of Toronto. It is easy to get from UTM to down town Toronto as there is a shuttle bus that students can take free of charge between UTM and the city. The UTM campus boasts a vibrant research environment and many of its faculty and researchers are recognized internationally for their innovative work.
